reason: The item cleanly targets boundary-aware interpretation of SOC-dependent vent-gas signatures, separating supported “suppression/relative hazard reduction/energy availability” claims from unjustified absolutes (“proves,” “entirely,” “cannot”). Decoys are engineering-plausible and mostly single-fault, though two rely on similar absolute-overclaim structure, slightly weakening discriminative spread. Overall, the question forces experts to avoid overinterpreting composition fractions into mechanistic certainty or zero-risk safety conclusions.

The prompt tightly specifies the SOC-dependent propagation intervals, gas quantities, and the explicit boundary condition that propagation ceases only below 25% SOC in closed space, enabling a clean assessment of the engineer’s flawed equivalence claim. It strongly discriminates between candidates who recognize “slower” versus “non-propagating” safety standards and who can connect SOC to both heat release and gas-phase combustible fraction without over-claiming elimination of explosion risk at 50% SOC.
